coredata.dc:title: Thin-film nanocomposite membranes containing aspartic acid-modified MIL-53-NH2 (Al) for boosting desalination and anti-fouling performance
coredata.dc:creator: Bayrami, Arshad
Bagherzadeh, Mojtaba
Navi, Hossein
Chegeni, Mohsen
Hosseinifard, Mojtaba
Amini, Mojtaba
coredata.dc:description: In the current study, the prospect improvements on desalination and anti-fouling performance of polyamide (PA)-based TFN membranes modified with MIL-53-NH-Asp have been investigated. MIL-53-NH2 nanoparticles (NPs) have been treated through a single-step post-synthesis modification reaction to enhance the hydrophilicity feature and compatibility with the PA layer. Various concentrations of synthesized NPs were dispersed in an aqueous phase consisting m-phenylenediamine and 2,6-diaminopyridine monomers for incorporation in the PA rejection layer. Analysis data of fabricated membranes provide evidence of changes in their physico-chemical properties after NPs incorporation. In comparison with blank TFC membrane, the TFN-MAs3 membrane (with the optimal NPs loading value) improved the selectivity to a value of 0.19 g/l in AL-FS mode with 1 M draw solution concentration; owing to ca. 40% increase in water flux and minor decrease in reverse salt flux. Moreover, the optimal membrane with a lower flux drop and higher flux recovery value shows good resistance to fouling phenomenon compared to the pristine TFC membrane. The ultimate achievement of current study reveals the significant potential of MIL-53-NH2 in the PA layer in terms of improved forward osmosis performance of TFN membranes, particularly when they are designed for this application (modification by zwitterionic organic molecules).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the synthetic steps for MIL-53-NH-Asp NPs and (b) scheme diagram for TFN-MAsx membranes fabrication.

NPs characterization: XRD patterns of the MIL-53-NH-Asp, synthesized MIL-53-NH2 and simulated MIL-53-NH2; FTIR spectra of the L-Asp, L-Asp anhydride hydrobromide, MIL-53-NH2 and MIL-53-NH-Asp; N2 ad/desorption isotherms at -196 °C and BJH pore size distribution curves of MIL-53-NH2 and MIL-53-NH-Asp samples; FESEM images of the MIL-53-NH2 NPs before and after PSM process with two different magnification scales.

The calculated penetration and structural parameters of the pristine TFC and TFN-MAsx membranes.

head.ce:title: Thin-film nanocomposite membranes containing aspartic acid-modified MIL-53-NH2 (Al) for boosting desalination and anti-fouling performance

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the current study, the prospect improvements on desalination and anti-fouling performance of polyamide (PA)-based TFN membranes modified with MIL-53-NH-Asp have been investigated. MIL-53-NH2 nanoparticles (NPs) have been treated through a single-step post-synthesis modification reaction to enhance the hydrophilicity feature and compatibility with the PA layer. Various concentrations of synthesized NPs were dispersed in an aqueous phase consisting m-phenylenediamine and 2,6-diaminopyridine monomers for incorporation in the PA rejection layer. Analysis data of fabricated membranes provide evidence of changes in their physico-chemical properties after NPs incorporation. In comparison with blank TFC membrane, the TFN-MAs3 membrane (with the optimal NPs loading value) improved the selectivity to a value of 0.19 g/l in AL-FS mode with 1 M draw solution concentration; owing to ca. 40% increase in water flux and minor decrease in reverse salt flux. Moreover, the optimal membrane with a lower flux drop and higher flux recovery value shows good resistance to fouling phenomenon compared to the pristine TFC membrane. The ultimate achievement of current study reveals the significant potential of MIL-53-NH2 in the PA layer in terms of improved forward osmosis performance of TFN membranes, particularly when they are designed for this application (modification by zwitterionic organic molecules).

ce:section.ce:para: Human population outbursts, industry development and insatiable demands of society have affected the environment in a harmful way leading to a serious lack of potable water worldwide [1,2]. It has been reported that only 3% of accessible water is considered potable and is exposed to human-driven contaminations [3]. With that being said, treatment of contaminated water and desalination of seawater (which encompasses the most part of the accessible water sources) has become a crucial obstacle to scientists and engineers; leaving them in a struggle for the development of new technologies [4,5]. Many and different approaches have been put into effect and tried for water desalination. These processes can be classified into two general categories: 1) thermal-based processes such as vapor compression (VP), multi-effect distillation (MED) and multi-stage flash evaporation (MSF) and 2) membrane-based processes such as electro-dialysis (ED), reverse osmosis (RO) membrane distillation (MD) and forward osmosis (FO) [6,7]. Membrane-based methods outstand thermal methods in terms of energy consumption and overall costs, and currently hold a major part of the global water treatment technology such as the RO process [8,9]. In recent years, the FO method has become an interesting and efficient way of water treatment and desalination due to low energy consumption and less membrane fouling value compared to other alternative methods [9–12].

The development of ideal membranes is an active and competitive field of study because they are an inevitable part of a desalination system. Cellulose acetate/triacetate (CA/CTA) based membranes and TFC membranes are the most common types currently used in this technology [7,13]. CA/CTA type membranes possess high water flux, hydrophilicity, mechanical resistance, low fouling tendency and high resistance to chlorine and oxidant agent [14,15], however, they show negligible resistance to hydrolysis and biological incursion and obviously higher reverse salt flux [7,14]. Asymmetric TFC membranes have been designed and produced to overcome the drawbacks of symmetric CA/CTA membranes [1]. This type of membrane has one porous supporting layer with a thickness of 100–200 μm (generally with the purpose of providing mechanical resistance for the second layer) and one thin selective layer with a thickness of 0.1–1 μm (with the purpose of selective transformation of water molecules and solute rejection) [16–18]. It should be noted that TFC membranes with very thin PA layer (>50 nm), obtained through eclectic methods, namely surfactant assembly regulated interfacial polymerization, also are reported in nanofiltration applications [19,20]. An ideal TFC–FO membrane carries beneficial characteristics such as an active layer with high water permeation, low salt permeation and superior anti-fouling trait and a supporting layer with high mechanical resistance and low structural parameter. Therefore, studies on the development of TFC membrane (improvement of the aforementioned parameters) have been focused on both supporting and active layers [2,21,22]. Lately, miscellaneous additive compounds and polymers have been employed in order to improve FO membrane performance [23].

Nanoparticles with appropriate structural stability in water and high porosity are believed to be promising compounds for incorporation in FO desalination polymer membrane [24–26]. MOFs are seemingly excellent candidates for discussed application in this field owing to the above-mentioned features and also adjustable pore size by structural manipulations. Compared to pure inorganic materials, these compounds exhibit better compatibility with the polymer layer and reduce the possibility of undesired pore formation at the interface of NPs and polymer [27–29]. Among reported studies about utilization of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) for improving the FO membranes, UiO-66 and its derivatives take a major part of the studies and less attention is allocated to other compounds of this category such as the MIL family [29–33]. Lee and colleagues have employed F300, A100 and C300 which are MOFs based on Fe, Al and Cu metals, respectively, for the preparation of MOF-based porous matrix membranes (PMMs) to control the ICP phenomenon. By adding MOF nanoparticles in polyacrylonitrile (PAN) dope solution they were capable of increasing porosity and improving the membrane performance [34]. In another study by Wang and et al., the influence of MIL-53 (Fe) on FO functionality of CA membranes has been investigated [35]. Kadhom and colleagues reported the utilization of MIL-125 and UiO-66 for modification of TFC membranes; their results showed that both applied MOFs can improve the membranes' performance [36]. To the best of our knowledge, there has been no report about the utilization of MIL-53-NH2 (Al) MOF in an active layer of TFC for FO membrane. However, this compound possesses exclusive characteristics for incorporation in this layer. On top of all aforementioned features (water stability, porous structure and compatibility with polymer layer) these compounds benefit from pores with breathing traits [37]. This implies that large-pore (lp, with channel diameter of 7.3 × 7.6 Å2) in freshly synthesized MIL-53 (Al) transforms into narrow-pore (np, with channel diameter of 2.6 × 13.6 Å2) in presence of water at ambient temperature [38,39]. This event occurs because of hydrogen interactions between water molecules inside the pore and the linker carboxylate group and prepares pores for selective water transportation (in terms of size). Therefore, this compound can provide excessive channels for water transportation in the PA layer and bolsters up the selectivity feature. Numerous reasons are accountable for choosing MIL-53-NH2 (Al) such as low cost and convenient accessibility of the metal center (Al), holding amine group for playing part in connection of MOF to PA active layer and making MOF capable of post-synthetic modification (PSM) [38,40,41]. Modifications have been carried out to improve hydrophilicity by zwitterionic pair on its surface and to improve the compatibility with the PA layer.

In this paper, at the first step MIL-53-NH2 (Al) NPs physically connect to an aspartic amino acid in order to boost up their hydrophilicity and compatibility with the PA layer. Afterward, it was incorporated with different concentrations in the PA active layer during interfacial polymerization step and desalination performance of the fabricated membranes through the FO method was studied. After determining the optimal membrane with the most effective nanofiller loading value, the anti-fouling functionality of which in FO mode was investigated meticulously.

Polyethersulfone (PES) (Ultrason E6020, Mw: 58,000) was acquired from BASF Co. Trimesoyl chloride (TMC), 2,6-diaminopyridine (DAP), m-phenylenediamine (MPD), Polyethylene glycol 300 (PEG), aluminum chloride hexahydrate (AlCl3·6H2O), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4), monopotassium phosphate (KH2PO4), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), calcium chloride dihydrate (CaCl2.2H2O), ammonium chloride (NH4Cl), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and n-hexane were purchased from Merck Co. Phosphorus tribromide (PBr3), 2-aminoterephthalic acid (BDC-NH2), sodium alginate (SA), l-aspartic acid (l-Asp), acetone, acetonitrile and tetrahydrofuran (THF) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich Co. Sodium chloride (NaCl) was acquired from Iranian Mineral Salts Companies. All reagents were used as received without further purification except THF, which was dried over sodium before using.

According to a formerly addressed method, PBr3 and dried THF solvent were employed to dehydrate aspartic acid [42]. Since moisture can decompose anhydride structures, dryness of solvent is an influential factor for the successful synthesis of the intended compound. Concisely explaining, 1.33 g of l-Asp was added to 10 mL of THF at 0 °C and stirred for 10 min. In a subsequent step, 0.83 mL of PBr3 was added in a dropwise manner and heated to reach room temperature then stirred for 6 h. The obtained white precipitate was collected by centrifugation and then rinsed with dried THF three times, eventually left in a vacuum oven to dry thoroughly (Fig. 1a).

MIL-53-NH2 (Al) nanoparticles were synthesized according to a formerly published procedures in this regard [43,44]. Summarizing the approach, 3.1 mmol (0.76 g) of AlCl3.6H2O and 3.1 mmol (0.56 g) of BDC-NH2 were dissolved in a mixed solvent of DMF and water with 3:1 volume ratio and stirred for 2 h. The obtained mixture was transferred to a stainless-steel autoclave and remains for daytime at 150 °C. After cooling down, the yellow sediment was separated via centrifugation and rinsed with hot DMF for 6 h to remove unreacted linkers from pores. In the final step, the obtained precipitate was rinsed once more, this time with acetone three times and dried under vacuum at 120 °C temperature (Fig. 1a).

At first, MIL-53-NH2 (Al) was activated at 150 °C at least for 6 h to remove the trapped DMF molecules in the pore structure. Progressively, 0.25 g of MOF nanoparticles were dispersed for 15 min. in 25 mL dried acetonitrile; then 1.1 g l-aspartic anhydride hydrobromide were added through few steps under vigorous stirring and refluxed 36 h at 85 °C. After amassing the resulting solid by centrifugation, the obtained compound was rinsed 3 times with acetonitrile and water respectively and finally, it was dried at 80 °C for a daytime. The synthetic steps are summarized in Fig. 1a.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PES membrane substrates through phase inversion method
ce:section.ce:para: The PES support layer of FO membranes was prepared according to our previously reported procedure [45,46]. In brief, 2 g of PES powder (that previously dried for 24 h to eliminate remnant moisture) completely dissolved in a mixed solution of PEG (5.6 g) and DMF (8.4 g). After about 12 h the resulting solution was degassed over 24 h and the membrane substrate cast over a clean glass plate using a hand-casting knife with a gate height of 150 μm; and then soaked into a water bath to completely detach from the glass plate surface. The obtained PES membrane substrates were stored in deionized water for 48 h before than formation of a polyamide layer on the top surface of it.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of TFC and TFN-MAsx FO membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Experimental conditions regarding the fabrication of active PA layer and PA/NPs composite layer with various dosages via interfacial polymerization (IP) are summarized in Table S1. An aqueous solution consisting 1.25 wt% MPD and 0.75 wt% DAP monomers and organic (hexane) solution consisting 0.15 wt% TMC monomer was prepared by dispersion of the corresponding solution for 30 min. After fixing the PES substrate on a clean glassy plate, its surface was in contact with an aqueous solution for 2 min and after removing the excess solution, the same treatment was carried out again but this time with an organic solution lasting for 90 s. In order to complete the reaction between unreacted functional groups of two monomers and the formation of a condensed PA layer, the system was maintained in an oven at 65 °C for 7 min [47]. Finally, the fabricated membranes labelled as TFC and TFN-MAsx (in which the “MAsx” mean the presence of MIL-53-NH-Asp NPs with different content in the MPD-DAP aqueous solution) were kept in deionized water until tested. Schematic diagram of TFN-Masx FO membranes is illustrated in Fig. 1b.

All powder (NPs) and polymeric (membrane) samples were derided before characterization. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) of NPs in the 400–4000cm−1 wavenumber range was measured with a Unicam Matson1000 instrument using the KBr pellet method. Attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) spectra of the fabricated membranes were recorded using a Perkin Elmer Spectrum 100 FTIR spectrometer between the wavenumber range of 650–4000 cm−1. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the synthesized NPs and prepared membranes were obtained by XPERT-PRO diffractometer system (MPD PANalytical Company) in a range of 2θ = 5–80° using a Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) source. Membrane surface hydrophilicity tests were performed at room temperature via sessile drop contact angle measurement of deionized water on membrane surface using a contact angle goniometer (Dataphysics, OCA 15 plus). Images have been recorded at least at five random locations within 30 s of the drop deposition and the average values of them were reported. The surface topography of the fabricated membranes was investigated using atomic force microscopy (AFM, ENTEGRA AFMNT-MDT model) in tapping mode. The quantitative surface roughness is described as the average roughness (Ra) was obtained with a scanning area of 5 μm × 5 μm. The morphology and chemical compositions of the synthesized NPs and FO membranes were investigated by field emission scanning electron microscopy and scanning electron microscopy, respectively (FESEM, TESCAN MIRA3 model), equipped with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX). The samples were coated with a platinum or gold layer using a sputtering apparatus before analysis to enhance the conductivity of theirs. The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) and Langmuir surface areas and pore size distributions by the Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) method were ascertained by N2 ad/desorption data measured at −196 °C on a Belsorp-mini II instrument.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FO performance evaluation of the fabricated membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
The FO performance of as-fabricated TFC and TFN-MAsx membranes was evaluated by bench-scale FO setup at room temperature (Fig. S1). DI water and NaCl 0.5–2 M were employed as draw and feed solutions, respectively; pumped in opposite directions concerning each other on both faces of the membrane with a velocity of 200 ml/min. Weight change of DS and conduction of FS in both FO (i.e. active layer oriented toward FS, AL-FS) and PRO (i.e. active layer faced to DS, AL-DS) modes were recorded. The experiment was repeated three times for each membrane and average water flux (J
w , units of Lm−2 h−1 or LMH) and reverse salt flux (J
s , gm−2 h−1 or gMH) were determined using the following equations.

The intrinsic properties of the prepared FO membrane including A (water permeability coefficient, Lm−2 h−1 bar−1), B (salt permeability coefficient, Lm−2 h−1) and S (structural parameter, μm) were measured using the reported protocol [48]. Each experiment consists of four consecutive steps in which DI water and NaCl solution of various concentrations were employed as FS and DS, respectively. After calculations of water flux and reverse salt flux in each step, the obtained values fit to following FO transport equations via a least-squares non-linear regression.

The crystalline structure of prepared compounds was investigated by XRD diffraction analysis and corresponding patterns are illustrated in Fig. 2 . The XRD pattern of MIL-53-NH2 exhibits three index peaks in 2θ = 9.2°, 10.1° and 18.2° which are related to crystalline planes of (110) and (200) and combined planes of (211) and (220) [43,50]. The similarity between this pattern and those of simulated and MIL-53-NH-Asp samples verifies the successful synthesis of the desired compound and unchanged crystalline structure in the PSM step, respectively.

In order to identify and characterize the chemical bonds and existing functional groups in L-Asp anhydride bromohydride, MIL-53-NH2 (Al) and MIL-53-NH-Asp (Al) compounds, FT-IR spectroscopy was employed; the spectra are illustrated in Fig. 2. For MIL-53-NH-Asp corresponding peaks in 3386, 3499, 1672, 1398, 1581 and 1444 cm−1 are imputed to NH stretching vibrations of amine group, CO asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of carboxylic acid and CC vibration of a benzene ring, respectively [43,51-53]. By comparing the L-Asp and L-Asp anhydride bromohydride spectra, we can assert that disappearance of the intense peak in 1687 cm−1 (which is attributed to CO stretching vibration of aspartic acid) and also the appearance of three new peaks in 1778 and 1870 cm−1 (which is obviously related to cyclic anhydride group) are providing essential evidence about the successful synthesis of anhydride compound [54,55]. Two above-mentioned new peaks have been disappeared due to the reaction with MOF; indicative of anhydride ring-opening and its attachment to MIL-53-NH2. Moreover, the transition of aspartic peaks in relative range of 2500–3500 cm−1 (belonging to CH and OH bonds) to MIL-53-NH2 spectrum and decrease in peak intensity of NH2 group after PSM process provides further information on the linkage of aspartic to MOF [56]. Also, one must take into consideration that two peaks centered around 1149 and 3050 cm−1 are attributed to wagging and stretching vibrations of NH bond; implying the presence of amino acid in zwitterion form (NH3 +/CO2 −) [56]. Zwitterion materials are considered as powerful compounds to serve the function of improving water flux and anti-fouling performance of membranes.

In order to investigate the changes in specific surface area and porosity of MIL-53-NH2 after modification by ASP, N2 ad/desorption analysis and BJH pore size distribution curve were recorded for MIL-53-NH2 and MIL-53-NH-Asp compounds (Fig. 2). According to IUPAC classification, both curves lie in the common type I microporous materials (MOFs and zeolites). Based on adsorption-desorption curve, N2 sorption value for MIL-53-NH2 (SBET = 595.4 m2g−1 and SLangmuir = 689.0 m2g−1) is a little higher which is caused by its large specific surface area compared to MIL-53-NH-Asp (SBET = 522.8 m2g−1 and SLangmuir = 635.1 m2g−1). A decrease in BET and Langmuir-specific surface area after the PMS step has been reported in former studies as well. BJH curve perfectly characterizes microporous structure for prepared compounds. Both compounds are roughly free from meso- and macroporous structure and by progressing toward micro interval (<2 nm) curve begins to grow. Due to the partial occupation of MIL-53-NH2 pores by aspartic acid, the total pore volume of MIL-53-NH-Asp (Vtot = 0.36 cm3g−1) decreases compared to parent MOF (Vtot = 0.49 cm3g−1). However, the microporous structure of nanoparticles has remained unchanged and the possibility of unselective pore formation (modified MOF pores) for salt permeation in PA layer is negligible.

Fig. 2 illustrates FE-SEM images and EDX spectra for MIL-53-NH-Asp and parent MIL-53-NH2 nanoparticles. It can be deduced that MIL-53-NH2 (Al) NPs possess ellipsoid-like morphology and not surpass 100 nm in terms of size [43,44]. This morphology undergoes minor alterations through the PMS process and becomes semi-spherical by reducing the sharpness of corners of nanoparticles. It should be noticed in mind that the small size of MIL-53-NH-Asp NPs will be followed by proper distribution on the surface and better compatibility with the PA layer in logical loading percentages of these NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the fabricated FO membranes
ce:section.ce:para: ATR-FTIR analysis was put into service to determine the chemical structure of the skin layer (PA) of as-fabricated FO membranes and characterize the existing functional groups of those. Recorded spectra are illuminated in Fig. 3 . Absorption bands detected in 1577 cm−1 (νs, aromatic ring CC), 1489 cm−1 (νas CC), 1317 cm−1 (νas C-SO2-C), and 1240 cm−1 (νs CO) [57], are indicative of the corresponding peaks of PES supporting layer. The shoulder peaks in 1657 and 1544 cm−1 are attributed to stretching vibration of CO bond and bending vibration of NH bond of amide groups which are detectable in all analyzed samples due to the presence of PA rejection layer. The intensity of these peaks rises as a result of increasing dosage of nanoparticles which itself is brought by the formation of new amide bonds between NPs and utilized monomers (between NH2 of the linker with TMC and aspartic acid with MPD or DAP molecules) [22,49,58]. One must bear in mind that nanoparticles also have amide bonds formed in the PMS step inherently but due to the peak overlap with other MOF peaks are not detectable in the MIL-53-NH-Asp spectrum. Therefore, another reason regarding the cause of the increase in these peak intensity is triggered by the amide functional groups of NPs and can be considered as indicative of successful introduction of nanofiller in PA active layer. As illustrated in Fig. 4 , owing to low loading value and also covering of NPs peaks with those of PES/PA polymer, the characteristic peaks of nanofillers are not detectable in TFN-MAsx spectra.

The XRD diffraction analysis was employed to characterize the MIL-53-NH-Asp incorporated FO membranes (Fig. 3). All samples exhibit one broad peak centered roughly in 19° (2 theta) which is assigned to amorphous polymer. There has been no proof showing a drastic difference between TFC and modified membranes (up to 0.09 wt% of NPs) pattern due to low nanoparticle loading in the PA active layer. The only observed difference in the TFN-MAs4 membrane is one weak and sharp peak in 2 theta of 9.2° which is caused by (111) plane of MOF (circled region in the corresponding pattern). The presence of a specified peak verifies the existence of nanoparticles in the active layer.

The surface wettability of PES/PA-NPs membranes and NPs-free TFC membrane was revealed by water contact angle (WCA) analysis. According to results (Table 1), membranes surface hydrophilicity increases following the incorporation of nanofillers in the PA layer and shows lower WCA values compared to blank TFC membrane. The presence of introduced NPs with hydrophilic functional groups on the membrane surface is held accountable for the increase in WCA. Some of the defined functional groups of nanofillers which facilitate water molecules absorption on the membrane surface are amine and free carboxylate groups on MOF linkerformed amide bonds between MOF and Asp and most importantly zwitterion NH3 +/CO2 − pair caused by Asp amino acid (confirmed by FTIR data in Fig. 2). The selected WCA images of membranes are illustrated in Fig. 4 and TFN-MAs4 with 0.12 wt% loading value reveals the lowest water contact angle among all analyzed samples. The surface hydrophilicity is considered as an important property of membrane in terms of achieving an enhanced water flux and more favorable anti-fouling performance [31,32,59].

Corresponding data of modified and unmodified membranes surface roughness is expressed in Table 1 and related 3D topographic AFM images are illustrated in Fig. 4. Following the introduction of NPs to the extent of 0.00–0.06 wt%, Ra and Rq values decrease from 62.2 and 77.3 nm in TFC to 36.3 and 49.5 nm in TFN-MAs2 membrane, respectively. In the case of higher loadings, the trend is reversed and the average roughness parameter increases again to reach a value of 47.0 nm in the TFN-MAs4 membrane. This increase is triggered by high concentration of nanoparticles on the surface. However, the TFN-MAs3 membrane surface becomes rougher following an increase in NPs concentration, the surface yet remains smoother than TFC membrane. The aforementioned effect (softness of membrane surface) is adopted as another influential factor in the fouling phenomenon and reduces precipitation sites on the surface [60,61].

The surface morphology of TFN-MAsx membrane incorporated with different dosage of nanoparticles and control TFC membrane was determined by utilizing SEM and AFM analysis. According to SEM images illustrated in Fig. 4, the PA layer has been formed successfully in all membranes and bears a mixed common structure of ridge-and-valley and leaf-like patterns [59,62]. In comparison with PA layer structure of TFC membrane, increasing nanofiller concentration first leads to a smoother surface on membrane (TFN-MAs1 and TFN-MAs2 membranes) and at very high concentrations rougher membrane surface is formed eventually (TFN-Mas3 and TFN-Mas4 membranes). 3D-AFM images provide further evidence supporting the stated facts. A decreasing trend of ridge height and valley depth in polyamide structure turns out to be TFC > TFN-MAs1 > TFN-MAs3 > TFN-MAs4 > TFN-MAs2. Smooth membrane surface is one of the effective parameters in the reduction of membrane fouling performance. Energy Dispersive X-Ray (EDX) analysis was put into service to shed light on the presence of MIL-53-NH-Asp NPs on the surface of TFN-MAsx membranes. The appearance of Al peak in EDX elemental spectra of modified membranes provides reliable evidence about successful incorporation of nano-modifiers in PA rejection layer. It is noteworthy to mention that by contemplating lower parts of Fig. 4 (the EDX spectra), one can assert that as a result of an increase in nanofiller concentration in the active layer, Al peak intensity faces a spike.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of MIL-53-NH-Asp loading on the FO performance and intrinsic separation properties of membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
As a common condition in the FO test, NaCl 1 M solution and DI water were employed as draw and feed solutions to determine the water flux and reverse salt flux of the fabricated membranes in FO and PRO modes. Results from osmosis water flux are depicted in Fig. 5a. As it can be seen from this figure, water flux of membranes undergoes an enhancement that is evoked by the increase in MIL-53-NH-Asp nanofillers loading to a value of 0.09 wt%, however higher loading values up to 0.12 wt% decrease the flux in both operational modes. Contemplating Table 1 and 2 , these high water flux values of TFN-MAs3, in comparison with other tested membranes under analogous conditions can be assigned to low structural parameter (S) and high hydrophilicity of it [47,63]. On that account, in comparison to membrane containing 0.06 wt% of NPs, the rougher surface of TFN-MAs3 extends the effective contact area between water molecules and positively affects the J
w
[57,64]. Water flux decrease in case of higher concentrations/loading (TFN-MAs4) can be attributed to less increase in excessive water channels (MOF pores) due to nanofillers agglomeration and possible blockage of pores on the surface of PES supporting layer. Higher water flux values in PRO mode compared to FO mode are brought by the less internal concentration polarization in this orientation. Under identical experiment conditions (in terms of membrane orientation and DS concentration) TFN-MAs3 membrane reveals much higher J
w (22.8 and 35.4 LMH in FO and PRO modes, respectively) in comparison with all other fabricated membranes.

Reverse salt flux of tested membranes in the forward osmosis process is shown in Fig. 5a. Under analogous test conditions, reverse salt flux in the loading range of 0.00–0.09 wt% decreases slowly and then suddenly rises at loading of 0.12 wt%. According to the specific structure of MIL-53-NH2 NPs (owning pores with breathing effect), at the performed experimental condition this compound transforms its lp structure (with channel diameter 7.3 × 7.6 Å2) to np (with channel dimension of 2.6 × 13.6 Å2) and prevents from the introduction of hydrated salt ions into the pores (Na+ with a size of 7.16 Å and Cl− with a size of 6.64 Å) [38,39]. The outcome of this phenomenon is selective permeation of water molecules with a size of 2.76 Å by nanoparticle pores. With that said, up to 0.9 wt% loading value a minor decrease in reverse salt flux values of modified membranes is observed. An instant increase in the J
s value of TFN-MAs4 is perhaps the outcome of nanofillers aggregation and formation of unselective pores in NPs and PA active layer interface. Roughly similar values of J
s for TFC, TFN-MAs1, TFN-MAs2 and TFN-MAs3 membranes are informative about the complete and regular formation of the PA layer on PES substrate in these cases.

Fig. 5b illustrates the influence of MIL-53-NH-Asp nanofillers loading on the specific reverse salt flux (J
s /J
w ) of prepared membranes in both FO and PRO modes. This ratio is considered as an imperative parameter; low values of which show better performance of membrane. As b conveys, in a dosage range of 0.00–0.09 wt% due to the increase in J
w and minor decrease in J
s , the selectivity (J
s /J
w ) of the membrane increases significantly. Despite the drop in selectivity in the case of higher loadings (0.12 wt%) all modified membranes demonstrate less specific salt flux compared to unmodified TFC membrane. TFN-MAS3 membrane with J
s /J
w values of 0.191 and 0.197 g/L in FO and PRO modes, respectively, improves membrane selectivity up to 35% compared to pristine TFC in both modes. These results are illuminating the applicability and promising utilization of pre-modified MIL-53-NH2 in forward osmosis desalination membranes. For further investigations, the TFN-MAs3 membrane was selected as the optimal membrane owing to its highest water flux and selectivity under routine conditions of the forward osmosis test.

The influence of DS concentration on desalination performance of the optimal MIL-53-NH-Asp membrane in FO mode was evaluated and obtained data were compared to blank TFC membrane. Fig. 5c illustrate the graph regarding the J
w and J
s variations for these two membranes; DI water and NaCl 0.5–2 M were employed as FS and DS, respectively. According to graphs, following the increase in DS concentration, the osmosis pressure gradient between feed and draw solutions escalates and brings in higher values of J
s and J
w . Correspondingly, due to the low ICP in PRO mode, water flux and reverse salt flux values are higher compared to FO mode. This result is true in various tested concentrations; similar to when 1 M draw solution is engaged in the system.

With regard to the previously suggested method, structural parameters, pure water penetration coefficient and salt penetration coefficient of fabricated membranes were determined and results are summarized in Table 2. According to the results, we can observe that nanofillers incorporated membranes possess higher A and B coefficients values compared to control TFC membrane. The highest A value among tested membranes equals 2.368 LMHbar−1 and belongs to the TFN-MAs3 membrane. This increase is evoked by excessive water channels of the membrane that are provided by MOF pores. Despite the increase in B values at different loadings of nanofillers (TFN-MAsx), the B/A ratio (a characteristic of membrane selectivity) is less than the pristine TFC membrane due to higher A values. The TFN-MAs3 membrane with B/A ratio of 0.164 bar exhibits the best selectivity to water molecules. Additionally, the S value is 370 μm; the lowest value among fabricated membranes which is indicative of an increase in water transfer channels (also supported by high values of A) and a decrease in water transfer path [65–67]. To achieve higher water flux, membranes with low S values are being considered as proper candidates due to the less ICP they cause. Spike in S value for TFN-MAs4 contributes to pore blockage (reducing porosity) and tortuosity increase that is caused by nanofillers (S = thickness × tortuosity / porosity) [68]. The experiment results in this section show that the introduction of MIL-53-NH-Asp nanofillers in the active polyamide layer remarkably lessens the resistance to penetration with noticeable selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of MIL-53-NH-Asp loading on the anti-fouling performance of the membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Anti-fouling performance of TFN-MAs3 and pristine TFC membranes in the presence of model organic and protein foulants was investigated. This dynamic experiment was conducted in FO mode by employing NaCl as DS and synthetic wastewater containing 250 ppm SA and BSA as FS; results are summarized in Fig. 6 . The downward steep of the graph throughout the pre-determined time interval is indicative of a decline in water flux following the contamination of the surface by foulant agent. As it can be observed in the graph, in the presence of SA foulant, water fulx drops more rapidly and more intensely. Because Ca2+ ions act as a bridging unit between SA molecules and form a gel layer of SA which expedites the surface occupation by foulant [69]. As it is evidenced by Fig. 6, normalized water flux drop within 12 h for TFN-MAs3 (about 24% and 18% for SA and BSA, respectively) is lower than TFC control membrane (about 43% and 36% for SA and BSA, respectively). These results reveal that TFN-MAs3 membrane has much less tendency to decrease water flux in the presence of BSA and shows a better anti-fouling property. Higher hydrophilicity of TFN-MAs3 compared to pristine TFC membrane makes the water molecule absorption easier and facilitates the formation of a thin film water layer on the surface which prevents the adhesion of hydrophobic foulant to the surface [31,69]. The low wrinkled surface (smoother) of the TFN-MAs3 membrane is another auxiliary trait that improves its fouling resistance ability [60,61]. This event occurs by reducing the possibility of foulant agent deposition on the membrane surface following the valley depth reduction. Having conducted the fouling experiment, membranes have been washed with fresh DI for half an hour to determine the flux recovery ratio. As implied by the figure, normalized water flux for membranes increases by different percentages after washing, insinuating the reversibility of the fouling phenomenon. TFC control membrane with water flux recovery of 78% and 82% towards BSA and SA foulants, respectively, show higher irreversibility; due to the robust adhesion of foulant to surface. However, thanks to the smooth surface, lack of bearing site and adhesion area for foulant, modified membrane shows higher flux recovery value of 96% and 93% for SA and BSA, respectively. Higher hydrophilicity of TFN-MAs3 membrane leads to less interaction of foulant with surface and easier washing of foulant layer from the surface. The overall results of this section imply less fouling and its reversibility for optimal TFN-MAs3 membrane compared to the blank TFC membrane.

At last, to reveal the competency of the MIL-53-NH-Asp additives, the FO desalination results of the optimal TFN-MAs3 and blank TFC membranes in the current study were compared to as-prepared membranes incorporated with diverse NPs and commercial ones (Table S2). Owing to exclusive properties of employed nanofillers (having various hydrophilic functional groups and breathing pores with specific size), J
w for the optimal membrane is higher compared to most of the samples (rows 4–10) and lower reverse salt flux is observed (rows 2,3,5,6,8,9,10). These improvements in J
w and J
s values are followed by an enhancement in J
s /J
w for membranes modified with MIL-53-NH-Asp. As it can be seen in Table S2, the optimal membrane employed here is more selective than other membranes listed in the table and modified with different NPs. The obtained results reveal the high potential of our designed NPs for introduction in FO desalination membranes.

In this report, thin-film nanocomposite membranes incorporated with MIL-53-NH-Asp NPs were developed by interfacial polymerization of TMC organic phase and MPD-DAP/NPs aqueous phase on PES substrate surface. The presence of MIL-53-NH-Asp additives in the active PA layer has favorably increased hydrophilicity and enhanced inherent separation features of membrane compared to unmodified TFC membranes. In FO mode, optimal NPs loading of 0.09 wt% exhibits the best separation performance with the highest J
w of 22.8 LMH and the lowest J
s and specific reverse salt flux of 4.26 gMH and 0.191 g/L, respectively, when NaCl 1 M is used as DS. Furthermore, as-fabricated TFN-MAs3 membrane reveals significant anti-fouling performance toward SA and BSA model foulants, which can be attributed to the formed anti-adhesive hydrated layer and its less rough surface compared to the TFC membrane. The applicability of MIL-53-NH-Asp incorporated membranes in the FO process is fairly substantiated by improved anti-fouling feature and separation performance.

A. Bayrami: Original idea, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Project administration, Methodology, Writing - editing. M. Bagherzadeh: Original idea, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Review - editing. H. Navi: Methodology, Data curation, Writing - editing. M. Chegeni: Methodology, Data curation. M. Hosseinifard: Funding acquisition, Review - editing. M. Amini: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Review - editing.
